---
title: "4.8 size, add, and remove"
subtitle: "ArrayList and its Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## ArrayList Methods

### AP CSA reference methods

The `ArrayList` methods you need for the AP CSA exam include:

::: {.tight-list}
- `int size()` returns the number of elements
- `boolean add(E obj)` appends `obj` to the end and returns `true`
- `E remove(int index)` removes the item at the index and shifts remaining items left
:::

## ArrayList Methods

### Insert, access, and replace

::: {.tight-list}
- `void add(int index, E obj)` inserts at an index and shifts current objects right
- `E get(int index)` returns the item at the index
- `E set(int index, E obj)` replaces the item at the index
:::

`E` stands for the type of the element in the `ArrayList`.

## size()

### Number of items in the list

You can get the number of items in an `ArrayList` using its `size()` method.

The `ArrayList` starts out empty with a size of `0`.

```java
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();
System.out.println(list.size());
```

With arrays, use the `length` field. With an `ArrayList`, use the `size()` method.

## add(obj)

### Append to the end

You can add values to an `ArrayList` using `add(obj)`.

This adds the object to the end of the list.

The source compares this to joining the end of the line to board a bus.

The following code has a `String` list and an `Integer` list.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: listAdd`

Can you add another item to the shopping list and print out the new list?

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class Shopping
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<String> shoppingList = new ArrayList<String>();
        System.out.println("Size: " + shoppingList.size());
        shoppingList.add("carrots");
        System.out.println(shoppingList);
        shoppingList.add("bread");
        System.out.println(shoppingList);
        shoppingList.add("chocolate");
        System.out.println(shoppingList);
        System.out.println("Size: " + shoppingList.size());
        ArrayList<Integer> quantities = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        quantities.add(2);
        quantities.add(4);
        System.out.println(quantities);
    }
}
```
:::

## listAdd Test Target

### Runestone check

The original output is:

```text
Size: 0
[carrots]
[carrots, bread]
[carrots, bread, chocolate]
Size: 3
[2, 4]
```

The test expects changed output after another shopping-list item is added and printed.

## Autoboxing in ArrayLists

### int to Integer

Primitive types like `int` and `double` are automatically converted to `Integer` and `Double` using autoboxing when added to an `ArrayList`.

When you pull an `int` value out of a list of `Integer`s, that is called **unboxing**.

```java
ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<Integer>();
list.add(new Integer(5)); // this will work in Java 7
list.add(5); // this will work in all Java versions
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: StudentArrayList`

An example of an `ArrayList` of `Student` objects.

Add a new student with your name and info in it.

## StudentArrayList Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class StudentList
{
    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<Student> roster = new ArrayList<Student>();
        roster.add(new Student("Skyler", "skyler@sky.com", 123456));
        roster.add(new Student("Ayanna", "ayanna@gmail.com", 789012));

        System.out.println(roster);
    }
}

class Student
{
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private int id;

    public Student(String initName, String initEmail, int initId)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
        id = initId;
    }

    // toString() method
    public String toString()
    {
        return id + ": " + name + ", " + email;
    }
}
```
:::

## StudentArrayList Test Target

### Runestone check

Original output:

```text
[123456: Skyler, skyler@sky.com, 789012: Ayanna, ayanna@gmail.com]
```

The exercise prompt asks students to add a new student with their own name and information.

## add(index, obj)

### Insert and shift right

There are two different `add` methods in the `ArrayList` class.

`add(obj)` adds the passed object to the end of the list.

`add(index, obj)` adds the passed object at the passed index, but first moves existing values to higher indices to make room.

The indices for an `ArrayList` start at `0` and end at the number of elements `- 1`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: listAddInt2`

What will the code below print?

Try figuring it out before running it.

Remember that `ArrayList`s start at index `0` and that `add(index, obj)` always has the index as the first argument.

## listAddInt2 Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*; // import all classes in this package.

public class listAddInt2
{
    public static void main(String[] arts)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        list1.add(1);
        System.out.println(list1);
        // adds the number 2 to the end of the list
        list1.add(2);
        System.out.println(list1);
        // This will add the number 3 at index 1
        list1.add(1, 3);
        System.out.println(list1);
        // This will add the number 4 at index 1
        list1.add(1, 4);
        System.out.println(list1);
        System.out.println(list1.size());
    }
}
```
:::

## listAddInt2 Expected Output

### Runestone check

```text
[1]
[1, 2]
[1, 3, 2]
[1, 4, 3, 2]
4
```

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qalAdd1`

What prints?

```java
ArrayList<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<Integer>();
list1.add(1);
list1.add(2);
list1.add(3);
list1.add(2, 4);
list1.add(5);
System.out.println(list1);
```

Correct answer: `[1, 2, 4, 3, 5]`.

`add(2, 4)` puts `4` at index `2` and first moves `3` to index `3`.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qalAdd2`

What prints?

```java
ArrayList<String> list1 = new ArrayList<String>();
list1.add("Anaya");
list1.add("Layla");
list1.add("Sharrie");
list1.add(1, "Sarah");
System.out.println(list1);
```

Correct answer: `["Anaya", "Sarah", "Layla", "Sharrie"]`.

## remove(index)

### Remove and shift left

You can remove values from an `ArrayList` using `remove(index)`.

It removes and returns the item at the given index.

This moves all the other items over in the underlying array and decreases the size of the `ArrayList` by `1`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: listRem`

What will the following code print out?

Try to guess before you run it.

```java
import java.util.*; // import all classes in this package.

public class listRem
{
    public static void main(String[] arts)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        list1.add(1);
        list1.add(2);
        list1.add(3);
        System.out.println(list1);
        list1.remove(1);
        System.out.println(list1);
    }
}
```

## listRem Expected Output

### Runestone check

```text
[1, 2, 3]
[1, 3]
```

`remove(int index)` removes the object at the given index and shifts left any values to the right of that index.

It does not remove the object that matches the integer value given.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qListRem`

What prints?

```java
List<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<Integer>();
list1.add(1);
list1.add(2);
list1.add(3);
list1.remove(2);
System.out.println(list1);
```

Correct answer: `[1, 2]`.

The `3` at index `2` is removed.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- use `size()` to get the number of elements in an `ArrayList`
- use `add(obj)` to append to the end
- recognize autoboxing when adding `int` values into `ArrayList<Integer>`
- use `add(index, obj)` to insert and shift later items right
- remember `ArrayList` indexes start at `0`
- use `remove(index)` to remove by position, not by matching value
:::

## End

### 4.8 Part 2 complete

Next: get, set, and arrays versus ArrayLists.
